steps.title: Choose the right model shape
steps.content: The scatterplot has an increasing curved pattern: as $x$ increases, $y$ increases faster and faster. That is not linear behavior, so a straight-line model is not the best choice.

steps.title: Eliminate the straight lines
steps.content: Choice A is a line that slopes up, but it cannot follow the accelerating curve and most points are on one side of it. Choice B is even worse because it slopes downward while the data trend upward. Eliminate A and B.

steps.title: Check residual balance
steps.content: For a good model, the data points should be split reasonably above and below the curve. Choice C has all $8$ points below the curve, so it is systematically too high and not a good fit.

steps.title: Pick the balanced curve
steps.content: Choice D is an increasing curve and has points both above and below it: $2$ on the curve, $3$ above, and $3$ below. That balanced residual pattern makes D the most appropriate model, so choice D is correct.
